text: the shards, he saw Utak turn toward Rickard. He didn’t have much time. Ergant swallowed, faced Aurel. “Why did you bring me here?” “To find out the truth. Did you have anything to do with the attacks on Tregard’s ships?” “I told you, I—” But one of the shards flared and in it Ergant was handing over a purse to the captain of a ship. The captain reached into the pouch and pulled out a fistful of coins, letting them fall back into the sack as behind him his crew broke open a crate and snatched at the cutlasses inside. A few more shards flared, showing similar scenes, different captains, different ships, Elleron soldiers drilling the pirates in sword work, in attack patterns, in strategy. Farther away, more shards depicted those same ships on the open sea, hunting other ships. Cannons fired with bursts of fire and smoke. Hulls were breached, decks boarded. Tradesmen were gutted without mercy. Blood flowed like the sea. Ergant’s breath caught at the images, until he faced Aurel again. “I contacted certain pirates and struck a deal—money and weapons and training in exchange for the targeting of Tregard trade vessels.” “What about the villages?” Ergant shook his head, but the shards had already reacted, the images shifting to the raids. Aurel took a step towards one for a better look, breath held, thinking it might be Istabol. One of the rotating shards sliced across his arm, cutting through cloth and skin, edge as fine as paper. Blood welled. The sting was sharp, like a papercut, but not as unsettling as the sensation of jagged slashes that came from entering this world, steadily sinking deeper, into muscle, moving toward bone, as if he were being flayed alive. His gaze jumped from shard to shard, watched fisher families being hauled from their huts, children crying, women shrieking, many breaking free to run as the pirates began to cut them down. “I—” Ergant’s jaw clenched. “I never told them to hit the villages. They did that on their own.” “But you were complicit. You didn’t stop them once it started.” Ergant sidestepped some of the shards. “No, I didn’t. Even after reports reached me of what they were doing.” Aurel moved toward him, sudden and intent. “What about Istabol?” Ergant shook his head. “I know nothing—” “What about Istabol!” Aurel spun away from Ergant, searched the shards, but they were all blank, reflecting only this shattered world and their own faces. And then, abruptly, Utak appeared, shoving a struggling Rickard before her. Aurel’s king stumbled and fell to the ground composed of slivers of glass, cried out as they sliced into his hands, his knees. He caught himself, then staggered upright, hands shaking, splinters of glass jutting from his palms. A floating shard nicked his neck and he lurched to the side with another cry. “What is this place?” he gasped, gaze jerking around at the shards, at the blood dripping from his hands, at Ergant and Aurel. When they landed on Utak, he roared, “What is this place!” Utak ignored him, stepping up to Aurel’s chest. “How dare you bring my liege to T’giskall . It is a sacred place, a hellish place, not to be tread by men.” Rickard had limped to Aurel’s side, hands held protectively before him. Blood had begun to stain his breeches at the knees and Aurel could now see shards embedded there as well. Yet Rickard straightened, gathered dignity and arrogance and rage around him like a cloak. “What is she talking about, Aurel?” “We aren’t supposed to be here,” Aurel answered. “We were never meant to stay, only pass through when using the mirror portals. That was the agreement. Few of the mirrorwrights are even aware of this place.” Then he spoke to Utak. “But it was the only way to know the truth.” Utak’s expression hardened. “And have you found your truth?” Aurel glanced at Ergant. “I have. He arranged for the attacks on our trading ships. They were done by pirates, but in the employ of Elleron, including the raids on our ports and villages.” “I knew it!” Rickard tilted his head back and laughed, then pointed a bloody finger at Ergant. “I knew you were behind it, even though I couldn’t prove it. There was no evidence. But now you will pay. You will pay with land and goods and trade lanes. Tregard will own the southern Isles!” Something sounded beneath the steady hiss of breaking glass—a crackle, as of glass scraping against glass; a crunch, as of a mirror stepped on underfoot. Utak reached out to grip Aurel’s arm, fingers so tight his skin turned white. “We have to go.” But Aurel had latched onto what Rickard had said, his focus now on his king. “No evidence? But what of the oak leaf cloth I found in Elean’s hand?” Rickard pulled back from Ergant, his expression flickering from shock to vacancy, eyes shuttered closed. “I—it was—the Tregard soldiers—” The shards around him flared and Aurel shifted closer in order to see. The first showed Istabol, the villagers interrupting their daily chores as strangers entered the village. But not from the sea. These men came from inland. Soldiers. Tregard soldiers. In the next, the villagers were being summoned to the village docks, gathered into a ragged clump, confused, the bewilderment shifting to anger as the soldiers became rough, dragging a pregnant woman from her hut and tossing her to the ground at the edge of the group. Aurel glanced at the next shard and the next, the story unfolding in snippets of action and flares of light. Elean stepping from the crowd in affront, demanding to know what was happening, the soldiers’ commander ignoring her. He could imagine her declaring who she was, her importance to the court as the mirrorwright’s wife. But the commander didn’t falter.
